mnemonics.title: Design Thinking Stages
mnemonics.content: EDIPT - Empathy Drives Innovative Problem Solving Together. Remember: E-D-I-P-T flows like water through all stages cyclically.

mnemonics.title: Double Diamond Phases
mnemonics.content: D³C² - Discover, Define, Develop, Deliver. First diamond: Explore (Discover + Define). Second diamond: Evolve (Develop + Deliver).

mnemonics.title: Empathy Map Components
mnemonics.content: SADA - Says, Does, Asks, Thinks. These four quadrants help capture complete user understanding.

acronyms.usage: Problem statement defining user need, insight, and designer response
Question format to reframe problems as design opportunities
Simplest version of product to test core assumptions
The visual and interactive elements users interact with
Overall experience and satisfaction when using a product
Ideation technique to break mental blocks by generating terrible ideas first

visualTips.visualization: Picture a spiral or circle with five stations: Empathize (eye/heart), Define (magnifying glass), Ideate (lightbulb), Prototype (hammer/tools), Test (magnifying glass on users). The spiral shows iterative nature.

Two diamond shapes side by side: First diamond expands (discover problem space) then narrows (define). Second diamond expands (develop solutions) then narrows (deliver). Like an hourglass that blooms twice.
Four-quadrant grid: Top-left (Says - quotes), Top-right (Thinks - internal monologue), Bottom-left (Does - actions), Bottom-right (Feels - emotions). Picture a window divided into four panes.
Horizontal timeline with emotional valley and peaks. Y-axis = emotion (frustrated to delighted), X-axis = touchpoints/steps. Like a roller coaster showing user experience.

analogies.analogy: Like a chef tasting soup repeatedly while cooking - not waiting until the end to discover it's wrong, but iterating throughout the process.
Building a model house with cardboard before constructing the real home - quick, cheap, and easy to modify based on feedback.
Walking in someone else's shoes to truly understand their journey, challenges, and what matters to them.
Brainstorming is like fishing with a net - cast wide to catch as many ideas as possible before selecting the best ones.
Like sculpting - removing bit by bit to reveal the form, not trying to get it perfect in one attempt.
